teamObjective.explanation: I hold only low non-trump spades, so I should not commit my strongest card on an opening lead. Playing the 3 first preserves 4-6 for later tricks while probing whether my partner can cover.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner (player 2) likely has some higher cards or trumps, but I cannot assume specific ranks or suits. They may be willing to cover a low lead if it is cheap to do so, especially since no cards have been played.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent has 3 unknown cards. Because I hold no trumps and only low ranks, it is probable that at least one opponent can beat any single I lead, but I cannot identify which opponent has the higher cards or trumps.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 is the cheapest way to use my lead. It dumps my weakest card, keeps the 4-6 for future tricks, and gives my partner a chance to win the trick if they have a modest higher card. If an opponent takes it, I have lost almost nothing.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 would commit my highest card on a trick that opponents are still likely to beat with any trump or higher rank, leaving me with even weaker cards afterward.
The 5 is still a relatively valuable card in this weak hand; saving it for a later exchange or to cover a partner play is better than leading it now.
The 4 is only slightly stronger than the 3 and would be beaten just as easily, so it is better to preserve it and lead the 3 instead.

risks: An opponent with any higher single or trump can take the trick and keep the lead.
My partner may not be able or willing to cover the 3, letting opponents control the next lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card, I minimize resource loss and keep 4-6 available to recapture the lead or support my partner later in the round.
